stem:
The axolotl is a salamander found only in a few lakes near Mexico City. Unlike most amphibians, it never fully transforms into an adult form—it keeps its feathery gills and spends its entire life underwater. Scientists are especially interested in the axolotl because it can regrow lost limbs, parts of its heart, and even sections of its brain, a trait that is rare among vertebrates.

Which choice best states the main idea of the text?

choices.text: The axolotl lives in lakes near Mexico City.
Most amphibians transform into adult forms as they age.
Scientists study many kinds of salamanders.
The axolotl is an unusual amphibian that stays in a juvenile form and has remarkable regenerative abilities.

explanation: The passage emphasizes both the axolotl's failure to mature and its rare regenerative powers—together, these are what make it unusual and noteworthy.

stem:
Bioluminescent fungi glow a soft green in the forests of Brazil, Japan, and parts of the southern United States. The glow comes from a chemical reaction between a molecule called luciferin and oxygen in the fungus's cells. Researchers believe the light may attract insects, which then carry the fungus's spores to new locations and help it spread.

Which choice best states the main idea of the text?

choices.text: Luciferin is found only in fungi.
Bioluminescent fungi are the most common fungi in Brazil.
Insects are attracted to all brightly colored objects.
Some fungi produce their own light through a chemical reaction, and this glow likely helps them reproduce by attracting insects.

explanation: The passage explains how the glow is produced (chemical reaction) and why it matters (spore dispersal via insects)—together, that is the central idea.

stem:
Zora Neale Hurston was an American writer and anthropologist who grew up in Eatonville, Florida, one of the first all-Black towns in the United States. She traveled through the rural South in the 1930s collecting folktales, songs, and oral histories that might otherwise have been lost. Her novels, including Their Eyes Were Watching God, draw heavily on the speech patterns and traditions she documented in her fieldwork.

Which choice best states the main idea of the text?

choices.text: Hurston was the first person to collect American folktales.
Eatonville, Florida, is the setting of most of Hurston's novels.
Hurston wrote only about her hometown.
Hurston preserved Southern Black cultural traditions through fieldwork and used them as the foundation of her fiction.

explanation: The passage joins two points—her anthropological fieldwork and its role in her novels—into a single claim about her dual contribution.

stem:
The Svalbard Global Seed Vault, built into a mountainside on a remote Norwegian island, stores backup copies of seeds from crops grown around the world. If a war, natural disaster, or disease wipes out a country's own seed collection, the vault can send replacements. Since opening in 2008, it has already shipped seeds to Syria to help restore crops lost during the country's civil war.

Which choice best states the main idea of the text?

choices.text: Syria's agriculture depends entirely on the Svalbard vault.
Norway controls most of the world's crop seeds.
The seed vault was built to study arctic plants.
The seed vault acts as a global backup for crop diversity and has already been used to replace seeds destroyed in conflict.

explanation: The passage defines the vault's purpose (backup copies) and gives evidence it works (Syria)—the main idea captures both.

stem:
Jigoro Kano, a Japanese educator, developed the sport of judo in 1882 by adapting traditional jujutsu techniques. Kano removed the most dangerous moves so that students could practice them at full speed without injury. He believed judo should teach not only self-defense but also discipline and respect, and he insisted that students of all backgrounds be welcome in his school.

Which choice best states the main idea of the text?

choices.text: Jujutsu was banned in Japan before judo was invented.
Kano reshaped jujutsu into a safer sport intended to teach both physical and personal values to a broad range of students.
Kano's main goal was to make judo a competitive Olympic sport.
Judo is more dangerous than jujutsu.

explanation: The passage stresses Kano's safety changes, his values-based goals, and his openness to diverse students—one sentence must capture all three.

stem:
The Venus flytrap catches insects using a pair of leaves lined with tiny trigger hairs. When a bug touches two hairs in quick succession—or the same hair twice within about 20 seconds—the leaves snap shut in less than a second. This two-touch rule prevents the plant from wasting energy on raindrops or falling debris.

Which choice best states the main idea of the text?

choices.text: Venus flytraps grow faster than most other carnivorous plants.
Venus flytraps rely on a timing-based triggering system to catch real prey while ignoring false alarms.
Venus flytraps eat raindrops by mistake.
Venus flytraps close their leaves once every 20 seconds.

explanation: The central idea ties the trigger mechanism to its purpose—distinguishing real prey from debris—rather than just describing the snap.

stem:
In 1943, the mathematician Mary Jackson began working at NASA's predecessor agency, where she performed wind-tunnel calculations by hand. After years of doing the work of an engineer without the title, she petitioned the city of Hampton, Virginia, to attend night classes at a segregated school so she could qualify for promotion. In 1958, she became NASA's first Black female engineer.

Which choice best states the main idea of the text?

choices.text: Segregation in Hampton ended in 1958.
Mary Jackson overcame institutional barriers to earn NASA's engineering title she had already been doing the work of.
Wind-tunnel calculations were always done by mathematicians, not engineers.
NASA was founded in 1943.

explanation: The author's angle is on Jackson's persistence against segregation to claim the engineering role—her doing the work, fighting for the credential, then earning the title.

stem:
Ocean currents act like conveyor belts, moving warm water from the tropics toward the poles and cold water back again. The Gulf Stream, for instance, carries heat from the Caribbean all the way to northwestern Europe, keeping cities like London milder in winter than their northern latitude would suggest. Without this flow, much of Europe would experience winters more like those of Canada.

Which choice best states the main idea of the text?

choices.text: London and Canada are at the same latitude.
The Gulf Stream only affects the Caribbean.
Ocean currents distribute heat around the globe and significantly moderate the climates of distant regions.
Ocean water cools as it moves north.

explanation: The passage uses the Gulf Stream as an example of a larger claim about currents redistributing heat and shaping climate.

stem:
The common loon is best known for its haunting nighttime calls, but its physical adaptations are just as distinctive. Unlike most birds, the loon has solid rather than hollow bones, which makes it heavier and allows it to dive more than 200 feet below the surface in pursuit of fish. That same density, however, means the loon needs a long stretch of open water to build up speed for takeoff—on a small pond, it can become stranded.

Which choice best states the main idea of the text?

choices.text: Loons are slower fliers than most birds.
A loon's dense bones give it an advantage as a diver but create a tradeoff that limits where it can safely land.
Loons are famous mostly for their nighttime calls.
Solid bones are common in diving birds.

explanation: The passage explicitly frames the solid bones as both an asset (deep diving) and a liability (takeoff)—a tradeoff the main idea must capture.

stem:
In the 1960s, economist Elinor Ostrom began studying how communities managed shared resources like fisheries, grazing land, and irrigation systems. Many economists at the time assumed such resources would inevitably be overused and destroyed unless privatized or controlled by a central government. Ostrom's fieldwork, however, documented dozens of long-lasting local arrangements—some centuries old—in which users cooperated to set rules, monitor one another, and sanction cheaters without needing outside enforcement.

Which choice best states the main idea of the text?

choices.text: Ostrom's research challenged the assumption that shared resources must be privatized or government-run by documenting successful community-managed systems.
Most shared resources in the world today are managed by local communities.
Ostrom believed that privatization was always the best way to manage shared resources.
Irrigation systems are older than fisheries.

explanation: The passage sets up an assumption (privatize or govern) and shows how Ostrom's evidence undermines it—that contrast is the central idea.

stem:
The city of Curitiba, Brazil, is often cited as a model of urban planning. Rather than building an expensive subway system, planners in the 1970s designed a network of dedicated bus lanes with boarding stations where riders pay before the bus arrives, allowing buses to move almost as quickly as trains. The system was built at roughly one percent of the cost of a subway, and variations have since been copied in cities from Bogotá to Jakarta.

Which choice best states the main idea of the text?

choices.text: Curitiba's bus network is faster than any subway in the world.
Most cities in Latin America have abandoned subways in favor of buses.
Brazilian cities are generally faster to build transit than other countries.
Curitiba pioneered a low-cost bus-based transit design that rivals subway speeds and has been widely imitated.

explanation: The passage emphasizes three linked points—low cost, subway-like speed, and international copying—which together define the system's significance.

stem:
For decades, archaeologists believed that the people who built the massive earthworks at Poverty Point in present-day Louisiana must have been farmers, since no other hunter-gatherer society was known to have constructed such large-scale architecture. Recent excavations, however, have turned up no evidence of crops or farming tools at the site, only the bones of fish, deer, and wild plants. The findings suggest that the builders organized complex labor and monumental construction without ever switching to agriculture.

Which choice best states the main idea of the text?

choices.text: Archaeologists have not yet determined who built Poverty Point.
Hunter-gatherers in Louisiana grew small amounts of food alongside hunting.
Poverty Point is the largest ancient site in North America.
New evidence from Poverty Point contradicts the long-held view that large-scale monument building required a farming society.

explanation: The passage frames a prior assumption (monuments require farmers) and shows new evidence overturning it—a reversal that must be in the main idea.

stem:
The mantis shrimp has one of the most complex eyes in the animal kingdom, with up to sixteen types of color receptors compared to a human's three. Scientists once assumed this meant mantis shrimp could see an astonishing range of colors. Recent experiments, though, show the opposite: the shrimp are actually worse than humans at distinguishing similar shades. Researchers now believe the extra receptors allow the shrimp to identify colors instantly without comparing them—useful for a fast-moving predator that cannot afford to linger.

Which choice best states the main idea of the text?

choices.text: Mantis shrimp use their unusual eyes for speed of identification rather than fine color discrimination, contrary to earlier assumptions.
Mantis shrimp see more colors than any other animal.
Human vision is more complex than scientists realized.
Mantis shrimp cannot see color at all.

explanation: The passage replaces the intuitive assumption (more receptors = more colors seen) with a revised explanation (speed over discrimination)—that flip is the main idea.

stem:
The poet Gwendolyn Brooks became the first Black author to win the Pulitzer Prize for Poetry in 1950, at a time when most American literary magazines rarely published Black writers. Rather than use her rising fame to move into more elite circles, Brooks spent the later decades of her career teaching workshops in Chicago public schools, prisons, and community centers. She often said she wanted poetry to belong to ordinary readers, not just to critics and universities.

Which choice best states the main idea of the text?

choices.text: Brooks was the first woman to win the Pulitzer Prize for Poetry.
Chicago public schools rarely taught poetry before Brooks.
Brooks used her prize-winning status to reach ordinary readers and democratize poetry rather than to enter more elite literary circles.
Brooks stopped writing poetry after winning the Pulitzer Prize.

explanation: The passage contrasts Brooks's fame with her chosen audience—public schools, prisons, community centers—to stress her commitment to democratizing poetry.

stem:
In traditional economic models, a consumer is assumed to compare all available options and pick the one that maximizes personal benefit. Psychologist Herbert Simon challenged this view in the 1950s, pointing out that real people have limited time, memory, and attention. Rather than optimizing, Simon argued, we typically "satisfice"—we settle on the first option that meets a good-enough threshold and stop searching.

Which choice best states the main idea of the text?

choices.text: Economic models should include more variables to predict behavior.
Simon's concept of "satisficing" replaced the idea of fully informed optimizers with a more realistic account of limited human decision-making.
Consumers are lazier today than they were in the 1950s.
Simon believed economics should not study decision-making at all.

explanation: The passage sets up a baseline assumption (optimizing consumers) and describes Simon's correction—the central idea must name the correction itself.

stem:
The cane toad was introduced to Australia in 1935 in hopes that it would control beetles devastating sugarcane crops. Instead, the toads ignored the beetles—which lived higher on the plants than the toads could reach—and began spreading across the continent, poisoning native predators that tried to eat them. Today, the cane toad population numbers in the hundreds of millions and continues to push into new regions each year.

Which choice best states the main idea of the text?

choices.text: A planned biological control failed because cane toads couldn't reach their intended prey, and the toads became a greater ecological problem than the one they were meant to solve.
Cane toads are now Australia's most common amphibian.
Sugarcane beetles were eventually wiped out by native predators.
Australia banned imported species after 1935.

explanation: The passage hinges on an irony—the solution became the bigger problem—which the main idea must capture, not just one half of the story.

stem:
For much of the twentieth century, historians of medieval Europe focused almost exclusively on kings, bishops, and battles, relying on chronicles written in Latin by court scribes. Beginning in the 1970s, a wave of scholars turned instead to tax rolls, parish registers, and property disputes—sources that recorded the lives of peasants, merchants, and women who rarely appeared in official histories. These records did not merely fill in missing details; they often contradicted the chronicles, suggesting that the dramatic events emphasized by court scribes had little bearing on how most medieval people actually lived.

Which choice best states the main idea of the text?

choices.text: Medieval chronicles written in Latin are now considered unreliable on every subject.
Tax rolls and parish registers became available to historians only in the 1970s.
By turning to overlooked records, later medieval historians uncovered lives that were not only absent from traditional chronicles but also inconsistent with the picture those chronicles painted.
Historians since the 1970s have focused mainly on bishops rather than kings.

explanation: The central idea must capture both the new sources AND the crucial second claim—that they contradict, not merely supplement, the chronicles. (B) overreaches; (C) and (D) misread details.

stem:
Ecologists studying coral reefs have long treated healthy reefs as stable systems, assuming that a damaged reef will gradually return to its previous state once the source of stress is removed. Recent long-term studies complicate that picture. On several Caribbean reefs, even decades after pollution and overfishing eased, corals have not returned; instead, the reefs have been taken over by seaweed that crowds out coral larvae before they can settle. Researchers now describe such reefs as existing in an "alternative stable state"—a configuration that, once reached, may be as durable as the coral-dominated one it replaced.

Which choice best states the main idea of the text?

choices.text: Pollution and overfishing are the only causes of coral reef decline in the Caribbean.
Contrary to earlier assumptions, damaged reefs can lock into a new, seaweed-dominated state that persists even after the original stressors are gone.
Coral reefs always recover fully once human stressors are removed.
Seaweed is a more important component of Caribbean reefs than corals are.

explanation: The main idea must include both halves of the shift: the old assumption (reefs bounce back) and the new finding (they can lock into a different stable state). Only (D) captures that reversal.

stem:
Architect Lina Bo Bardi, who emigrated from Italy to Brazil after World War II, rejected the polished glass towers favored by many of her modernist peers. In projects like the São Paulo Museum of Art and the SESC Pompéia cultural center, she left concrete surfaces raw, exposed structural beams, and incorporated open plazas where visitors could gather without purchasing tickets. For Bo Bardi, a building's value lay not in the refinement of its materials but in how freely ordinary people could use the spaces inside it.

Which choice best states the main idea of the text?

choices.text: Bo Bardi's buildings are considered among the finest examples of Italian modernism.
The São Paulo Museum of Art charges no admission to any visitor.
Bo Bardi broke with the polished aesthetic of her modernist peers by designing buildings whose rough materials and open layouts were meant to invite public, unrestricted use.
Brazilian modernism replaced Italian modernism after World War II.

explanation: The passage joins two points: a rejection of polished modernism and a purpose rooted in public access. Only (A) captures both; the others distort details or overgeneralize.

stem:
Philosopher Hannah Arendt argued in the 1950s that what distinguished modern totalitarian regimes from earlier forms of tyranny was not simply their cruelty but their attack on the very idea of a shared public world. Traditional tyrants, Arendt observed, demanded obedience while leaving private life largely untouched; totalitarian governments, by contrast, sought to isolate individuals from one another so thoroughly that even a casual conversation with a neighbor felt dangerous. When citizens lost the ability to speak freely with one another, Arendt warned, they also lost the capacity to form the shared judgments on which meaningful political action depends.

Which choice best states the main idea of the text?

choices.text: Totalitarian regimes are typically less physically violent than earlier forms of tyranny.
Arendt believed that political action requires large public gatherings rather than private conversations.
Traditional tyrants had no interest in controlling their subjects' behavior.
For Arendt, what set totalitarianism apart was its destruction of ordinary social connection, which she saw as essential to citizens' ability to act politically together.

explanation: Only (C) unites Arendt's distinguishing criterion (destruction of shared social life) with its consequence (loss of collective political capacity); the others contradict or oversimplify the passage.
